answers.intake_q1: When did the wrist pain start?
answers.intake_a1: On and off for the past month
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what activities or movements make your wrist pain worse or better?
answers.intake_a2: writing for long makes it worse, rest makes it better
answers.intake_q3: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your hand or fingers?
answers.intake_q4: Can you describe which fingers or parts of your hand feel numb or weak?
answers.intake_a4: my pinky, ring finger, and part of my middle finger
answers.intake_q5: Any history of trauma or injury to your wrist before the pain started?
answers.intake_q6: Any weakness or difficulty with hand grip or finger movements?
answers.intake_q7: Any numbness or weakness in your arm or shoulder above the wrist?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, redness, or swelling around the wrist?
answers.intake_q9: Any history of diabetes, thyroid problems, or previous nerve issues?
answers.intake_q10: Any difficulty with fine finger movements like buttoning clothes or picking up small objects?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 54-year-old female presenting with on-and-off wrist pain for the past month. The pain worsens with prolonged writing and improves with rest. She reports numbness in the pinky, ring finger, and part of the middle finger, accompanied by weaker grip strength and some difficulty with fine finger movements such as buttoning clothes or picking up small objects. There is mild swelling around the wrist but no history of trauma, fever, or redness. She denies numbness or weakness above the wrist, including the arm or shoulder. There is no history of diabetes, thyroid problems, or previous nerve issues. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with ulnar nerve involvement at the wrist, likely due to ulnar neuropathy or compression at the Guyon's canal. The distribution of numbness in the pinky, ring finger, and part of the middle finger, along with weakness and swelling, supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Ulnar neuropathy at the wrist (Guyon's canal syndrome) 
2. Cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar nerve compression at the elbow) 
3. Cervical radiculopathy affecting C8-T1 nerve roots 
4. Early inflammatory arthritis or tenosynovitis causing swelling and nerve irritation 

P: 
- First-line medication: 
 NSAIDs such as ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed for pain and inflammation for 7-10 days. Avoid if contraindicated by allergy or gastrointestinal issues. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify. 

- Alternatives: 
 - Naproxen 250-500 mg orally twice daily for 7-10 days, if ibuprofen is not tolerated or contraindicated. 
 - Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain, if NSAIDs are contraindicated. 

- Medication safety considerations: 
 No known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant. No renal or liver disease reported. Use NSAIDs cautiously if gastrointestinal risk factors develop. 

- Return precautions: 
 Seek urgent care if there is worsening numbness, increasing weakness, severe pain, signs of infection (fever, redness, warmth), or inability to use the hand. 

- Testing: 
 If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., nerve conduction studies, urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome | TOS | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
rib and collarbone. You may feel burning, tingling, and numbness along your arm, hand, and fingers. If a nerve is compressed, you may also feel weakness in your hand. If a vein is compressed, your hand might be sensitive to cold, or turn pale or bluish. Your arm might swell and tire easily.
TOS is more common in women. It usually starts between 20 and 50 years of age. Doctors do nerve and imaging studies to diagnose it.
There are many causes of TOS, including:
Injury 
Anatomical defects 
Tumors that press on nerves 
Poor posture that causes nerve compression 
Pregnancy 
Repetitive arm and shoulder movements and activity, such as from playing certain sports 
Treatment depends on what caused your TOS. Medicines, physical therapy, and relaxation might help. Surgery may also be an option. Most people recover.
NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke

Carpal tunnel syndrome 
Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is pressure on a nerve in your wrist. It causes tingling, numbness and pain in your hand and fingers. You can often treat it yourself, but it can take months to get better.
Symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) 
The symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome include:
an ache or pain in your fingers, hand or arm numb hands tingling or pins and needles a weak thumb or difficulty gripping These symptoms often start slowly and come and go. They're usually worse at night.
How to treat carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) yourself 
Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) sometimes gets better by itself in a few months,

Broken arm or wrist 
Get medical advice as soon as possible if you think you have broken your arm or wrist. Any possible breaks need to be treated as soon as possible. 
Symptoms of a broken arm or wrist 
You may have broken your arm or wrist if you've injured it and it suddenly became:
painful swollen bruised difficult to move It may also change colour, change shape or feel numb.
It can be hard to tell if a wrist is broken, dislocated or badly sprained. You'll probably need an X-ray.
Urgent advice: Get help from NHS 111 if:
You've injured your arm or wrist and:
it's very painful, you cannot use it due to the pain, or the pain is getting worse there's

evidence.title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
, such as a sprain or broken bone 
Health conditions that can affect your nerves , joints , or other parts of your wrist, for example:
Diabetes . 
Rheumatoid arthritis . 
A cyst or tumor in your wrist. 
Changes in certain hormones , which may cause swelling from extra fluid in your body. Examples include an underactive thyroid gland (hypothyroidism), pregnancy and menopause. 
Problems with the way your wrist is formed. 
Doing the same wrist and hand movements over and over, such as: 
Assembly line work. 
Carpentry or using tools that vibrate. 
Knitting, gardening, or sports, such as golf. 
Researchers aren't sure whether long-term typing or computer use leads to carpal tunnel syndrome.
Who is more likely to develop carpal tunnel syndrome? 
Carpal tunnel syndrome usually happens only in adults. You're more likely to develop it if you:
Are a woman. Women are three times more likely to have
